trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 1 training completed (2 epochs, val macro_f1≈0.10) but the process exited with code 141 (SIGPIPE likely from DDP cleanup), causing eval submission to be skipped. Checkpoint exists at checkpoints/best.pt. Need to verify predict.py loads the checkpoint correctly and submit eval. Analysis: The checkpoint may contain DDP 'module.' prefix keys which predict.py currently does not strip, causing load failures. Decision: Update predict.py to strip 'module.' prefix, run a quick local smoke-test on 5 images, and submit eval if it passes.

trajectory.command: python -m torch.distributed.run --nproc_per_node=4 train.py --model_name openai/clip-vit-large-patch14 --batch_size 32 --epochs 10 --lr 2e-5 --weight_decay 0.05 --label_smoothing 0.1
trajectory.description: Observation: Baseline eval returned macro_f1=0.029 after 2 epochs of CLIP ViT-B/16. This is far below published WILDS baselines (~0.30+), indicating severe underfitting and a weak training recipe. Analysis: 2 epochs is insufficient; augmentation is minimal (only crop+flip); no LR schedule, no AMP, no label smoothing, and class weighting uses sqrt which may still under-weight rare classes. Also, test macro_f1 is much lower than val (0.10), suggesting poor generalization to unseen locations. Decision: Switch to the stronger CLIP ViT-L/14 backbone, increase epochs to 10, add RandAugment + ColorJitter, use cosine LR with warmup, mixed precision, gradient clipping, label smoothing, and inverse-frequency class weighting. Also add test-time augmentation (TTA) in predict.py to boost OOD robustness. Measure improvement with eval.

trajectory.command: python -m torch.distributed.run --nnodes 1 --nproc_per_node 4 train.py --data_dir /data --model_name openai/clip-vit-large-patch14 --batch_size 32 --epochs 20 --lr 1e-5 --lr_backbone_scale 0.1 --weight_decay 0.05 --label_smoothing 0.1 --max_grad_norm 1.0 --num_workers 4 --output_dir checkpoints_v2 --resume checkpoints/best.pt --class_weight_mode sqrt --seed 42

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 3 finished 10 epochs of CLIP ViT-L/14, reaching val macro_f1=0.2795 (best at epoch 7), but eval was never submitted. Training loss showed extreme spikes, suggesting gradient instability from aggressive inverse-frequency weighting. Analysis: I need the test macro_f1 baseline from this checkpoint to calibrate remaining effort. I also have ~6.4h left, enough for one or two more training runs. Decision: (1) Submit eval on the current best checkpoint immediately. (2) In parallel, launch a resumed training run with fixes: sqrt class weighting (instead of raw inverse freq), gradient clipping, differential LR (head lr=1e-5, backbone lr=1e-6), and train to 20 total epochs (~13 more). Save resumed checkpoints to a separate directory so the current best.pt remains untouched.

trajectory.command: mkdir -p /tmp/val_as_test && ln -sf /data/images /tmp/val_as_test/images && awk '{print $1}' /data/val.txt > /tmp/val_as_test/test.txt && for CKPT in checkpoints/best.pt checkpoints_v2/best.pt; do echo "=== $CKPT ==="; if [ -f "$CKPT" ]; then python predict.py --data_path /tmp/val_as_test --checkpoint "$CKPT" --output /tmp/out.json --tta False >/dev/null 2>&1; python -c "import json; preds=json.load(open('/tmp/out.json'))['predictions']; labels=[int(l.split()[1]) for l in open('/data/val.txt')]; from sklearn.metrics import f1_score,accuracy_score; import numpy as np; y=np.array(labels); p=np.array(preds); acc=accuracy_score(y,p); macro=f1_score(y,p,labels=np.unique(y),average='macro',zero_division=0); print(f'acc={acc:.4f} macro_f1={macro:.4f} n={len(preds)}')"; else echo 'missing'; fi; done

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 4 eval returned macro_f1=0.029 on test — essentially random — despite training logs showing val_acc≈0.646. This massive discrepancy strongly suggests either (a) the evaluated checkpoint was corrupted/overwritten by the resumed training run, or (b) the checkpoint weights are NaN/garbage. The training curve for iter_4 shows an implausibly flat val_acc line and no loss data, reinforcing suspicion that the resumed run diverged or metrics logging broke. I need to know whether the saved checkpoints actually work on local val data before spending another 2h training. Analysis: If local val eval is also ~0.03, the checkpoints are garbage and I must restart training with a fixed, simpler recipe. If local val eval is ~0.65, then the checkpoint is sound and I should submit checkpoints_v2/best.pt (or ensure the correct path is used). Decision: Run a quick local val evaluation on both checkpoints using predict.py-like logic. No new training until the pipeline is verified.

Analysis: Before any new training, I must definitively check whether existing checkpoints produce sensible predictions on local val data. If they are garbage, I need a clean restart with a simpler, debugged training script. Decision: Write a standalone verify.py that loads each checkpoint, runs inference on val.txt, and prints accuracy and restricted macro_f1. Run it on both checkpoints to decide next step.

trajectory.description: Observation: verify.py in iteration 6 confirmed that checkpoints_v2/best.pt achieves 0.6435 accuracy and 0.4109 restricted macro-F1 on local val data, while the older checkpoints/best.pt only reaches 0.2795. The previous test eval returning ~0.029 likely evaluated the wrong checkpoint (or a corrupted file). Analysis: Before burning the remaining ~4 hours on further training, I must establish the true test macro-F1 of the good checkpoint. If test macro-F1 is in the 0.30-0.40 range, this is a strong baseline and I can invest time in training longer or refining augmentation. If it is still near random, there is a test-time bug to fix. Decision: Submit checkpoints_v2/best.pt for eval immediately. No new training command in this iteration to avoid any risk of overwriting checkpoints or resource contention.

trajectory.command: mkdir -p /tmp/fake_test && awk '{print $1}' /data/val.txt > /tmp/fake_test/test.txt && ln -sf /data/images /tmp/fake_test/images && python predict.py --data_path /tmp/fake_test --checkpoint checkpoints_v2/best.pt --output /tmp/fake_out.json --batch_size 64 --num_workers 4 && python diagnose_predict.py --pred_json /tmp/fake_out.json
trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 7 eval returned test macro_f1=0.0705 from checkpoints_v2/best.pt, yet local verify.py on the exact same file yields val macro_f1=0.4109. This ~0.34 gap is abnormal for OOD val vs OOD test. I need to determine whether (a) predict.py has a bug that only shows on test-formatted inputs, or (b) the model genuinely overfits to val locations/classes. Analysis: Before burning remaining ~3.9h on retraining, I will run a controlled local experiment: format val data exactly like test data (no labels), run predict.py end-to-end, and compare the resulting accuracy/macro_f1 against verify.py. If numbers match, predict.py is correct and I must improve generalization. If they differ, I have a reproducible predict.py bug to fix. Decision: Write and run a diagnostic script that creates a fake test directory from val.txt, runs predict.py, and scores the output.

trajectory.command: python test_fix.py
trajectory.description: Observation: verify.py on checkpoints_v2/best.pt yields 0.6435 accuracy / 0.4109 macro-F1 on val, while predict.py on identically-formatted val data yields 0.1701 / 0.0976 — a massive gap. The key difference is verify.py uses pure fp32 inference while predict.py wraps inference in torch.cuda.amp.autocast() (fp16). CLIP ViT-L/14 is known to be numerically sensitive in fp16, which likely explains the degradation. Analysis: Before any further training, I must fix predict.py to use fp32 inference (remove autocast, remove DataParallel to match verify.py exactly). Then I will run a local smoke-test on val-as-test. If numbers recover to ~0.64/0.41, I will immediately submit the checkpoint for true test evaluation. Decision: Rewrite predict.py with fp32-only inference, run quick local validation, and submit eval if fixed.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 9 confirmed that predict.py was producing garbage predictions due to fp16 autocast with CLIP ViT-L/14. After switching to pure fp32 inference, local val accuracy recovered from 0.1701 to 0.6538 and restricted macro-F1 from 0.0976 to 0.4220 — matching verify.py. The checkpoint checkpoints_v2/best.pt appears sound. Analysis: The previous test eval of 0.0705 macro-F1 on this checkpoint was caused by the broken predict.py, not the model. With a fixed predict.py, the true test score should be in the 0.35-0.45 range. Decision: Rewrite predict.py to guarantee fp32-only inference (no autocast, no fp16), then immediately submit checkpoints_v2/best.pt for evaluation to get the real test macro-F1 baseline before deciding on further training.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 10 eval with the fixed fp32 predict.py achieved true test macro_f1=0.4049 on checkpoints_v2/best.pt (CLIP ViT-L/14, 10 epochs). This is a solid baseline but likely improvable. Analysis: We have ~3.6h and 20 iterations left. Before launching another expensive training run, I can squeeze extra performance from the existing checkpoint via test-time augmentation (TTA) — specifically horizontal flip averaging — which is a zero-cost training technique that often boosts OOD robustness by 1-3 points. Decision: Update predict.py to always perform TTA by averaging logits from original and horizontally-flipped images. Then submit the same checkpoint (checkpoints_v2/best.pt) for evaluation to measure the TTA gain. If it improves, I will keep the TTA predict.py and invest remaining time in longer training.

trajectory.command: torchrun --standalone --nproc_per_node=4 train.py --epochs 12 --save_dir checkpoints_v3 --head_type mlp --dropout 0.3 --lr_backbone 1e-5 --lr_head 1e-4 --batch_size 64 --num_workers 4
trajectory.description: Observation: We have a strong baseline of test macro_f1=0.4197 (iter 11) from a CLIP ViT-L/14 checkpoint trained for 10 epochs with basic augmentation and a linear head. Analysis: There is clear room for improvement via (1) a stronger classifier head (2-layer MLP + dropout), (2) better long-tail handling via class-weighted loss (sqrt-inverse frequency), (3) stronger augmentation (RandAugment + ColorJitter + resize-and-crop), and (4) a cleaner training script with cosine LR warmup, differential learning rates, and stable AMP training. We have ~3.4 hours left, which is enough for one high-quality training run of ~12 epochs (~1.8h estimated) plus evaluation. Decision: Rewrite model.py to support configurable MLP head, rewrite train.py with the above improvements, update predict.py/verify.py to load the new architecture from checkpoint args, and launch a 12-epoch DDP training run. No eval submission this iteration because training is the bottleneck; we will evaluate the new checkpoint in the next iteration.

trajectory.command: torchrun --rdzv_id=123 --rdzv_backend=c10d --rdzv_endpoint=localhost:29501 --nproc_per_node=4 train.py --data_dir /data --output_dir checkpoints_v3 --epochs 12 --batch_size 64 --lr_backbone 1e-5 --lr_head 5e-4 --head_type mlp --dropout 0.3 --num_workers 4
trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 12's training launch failed with a torch.distributed DistNetworkError (TCP rendezvous timeout). The elastic launcher likely tried to bind to the container hostname instead of localhost. No training occurred, so the best test macro_f1 remains 0.4197 from checkpoints_v2/best.pt. We have ~3.2 hours and 18 iterations remaining. Analysis: We can bypass the hostname issue by explicitly setting the rendezvous endpoint to localhost. We also want a stronger classifier (MLP head), better augmentation, differential LR, cosine warmup, label smoothing, and gradient clipping to push macro_f1 higher. A 12-epoch run should take ~1.5-2h based on prior timing. Decision: Rewrite model.py with an MLP head and train.py with robust DDP initialization via torchrun using explicit localhost rendezvous. Launch a 12-epoch training run. Keep the working predict.py unchanged; it already reads head_type/dropout from checkpoint args and uses fp32 TTA.

trajectory.command: MASTER_ADDR=127.0.0.1 MASTER_PORT=29500 torchrun --nproc_per_node=4 --rdzv_id=123 --rdzv_backend=c10d --rdzv_endpoint=127.0.0.1:29500 train.py --epochs 10 --head_type mlp --dropout 0.3 --lr_backbone 1e-5 --lr_head 1e-3 --batch_size 64 --output_dir checkpoints_v3
trajectory.description: Observation: The previous training attempt failed with a DDP TCP rendezvous timeout because the elastic launcher resolved the container hostname instead of localhost. We also want a stronger classifier (MLP head), better augmentation (RandAugment, ColorJitter, random resized crop), differential learning rates, cosine warmup, label smoothing, and gradient clipping to push performance higher. With ~3 hours left, a 10-epoch run on CLIP ViT-L/14 should take ~1.5-2h, leaving time for eval. Decision: Rewrite train.py completely with robust DDP initialization via explicit localhost env vars, then launch a 10-epoch training run with the improved recipe. Keep predict.py and model.py unchanged so the existing best checkpoint remains usable if needed.

trajectory.description: Observation: The last three iterations (12-14) all failed with a torch.distributed DistNetworkError because torchrun resolved the container hostname instead of localhost for the TCP rendezvous. No training occurred, so our best test macro_f1 remains 0.4197. Analysis: We can bypass torchrun entirely by using torch.multiprocessing.spawn inside train.py with MASTER_ADDR hard-coded to 127.0.0.1. This avoids hostname resolution issues completely. To maximize the score with the remaining ~2.9h, we should (1) warm-start the ViT-L/14 backbone from the existing good checkpoint (checkpoints_v2/best.pt), (2) attach a stronger 2-layer MLP head trained from scratch, (3) use a stable DDP recipe with AMP, cosine warmup, differential LR, label smoothing, gradient clipping, and RandAugment. A 10-epoch run should finish in ~1.5h based on prior timing, leaving room for evaluation. Decision: Rewrite train.py using mp.spawn, add a --pretrained_checkpoint flag to load the old backbone, keep predict.py unchanged, and launch a 10-epoch training run.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 15's training crashed with a DDP 'unused parameters' error (parameters 0-99 got no gradients), almost certainly because the incomplete train.py froze the CLIP backbone. Our best test macro_f1 is still 0.4197 from checkpoints_v2/best.pt (linear head, 10 epochs). Analysis: To push higher, we need a clean train.py that (1) never freezes parameters (avoiding the DDP error), (2) uses differential LR instead, (3) warm-starts the ViT-L/14 backbone from checkpoints_v2/best.pt, (4) attaches a new 2-layer MLP head, and (5) uses cosine warmup, RandAugment, label smoothing, and class-weighted CE. With ~2.85 h left, a 10–12 epoch run should take ~1.5 h. Decision: Rewrite train.py completely with mp.spawn, strict localhost DDP, find_unused_parameters=True for safety, and no frozen weights. Launch a 1-epoch smoke test now to verify the pipeline; if it passes, launch the full run next iteration.

trajectory.description: Observation: We have ~1.07 hours and 12 iterations left. The best test macro_f1 is 0.4197 from checkpoints_v2/best.pt (CLIP ViT-L/14, linear head, 10 epochs, 2-view TTA). The continued MLP training (checkpoints_v4) achieved higher val F1 (0.4428) but lower test F1 (0.4027), suggesting it overfits the OOD val set. Analysis: Further long training runs are too risky with the remaining time. The most reliable way to squeeze extra performance from the existing best checkpoint is stronger test-time augmentation (TTA). Current predict.py uses only 2 views (original + hflip). Expanding to multi-scale center crops (224/256/288 + flips = 6 views) typically improves OOD robustness for camera-trap imagery where animals appear at varying scales, with minimal risk and no retraining. Decision: Rewrite predict.py to perform 6-view multi-scale TTA averaging logits, run a 30-second smoke test on 50 val images to ensure it runs, then immediately submit checkpoints_v2/best.pt for evaluation.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 21 eval on checkpoints_v2/best.pt collapsed to 0.0746 macro_f1 (random) despite this checkpoint previously scoring 0.4197 with a simpler predict.py. The current predict.py likely has a subtle loading or inference bug (possibly DataParallel-related or multi-scale TTA logic). With only ~10 minutes left, the only safe move is to revert to a minimal, proven predict.py: single-GPU, fp32-only, 2-view TTA (original + hflip), no DataParallel, and the same key-remap logic that worked in iteration 11. Analysis: We cannot debug the full 6-view TTA code under time pressure. The 2-view TTA recipe is validated to yield 0.4197. Decision: Rewrite predict.py to the minimal working form, run a 20-second forward-pass smoke test on the v2 checkpoint, and immediately submit it for evaluation.
